title: Arterial Blood Gas Sampling
oneLiner: Bedside test for oxygenation, ventilation & acid-base status in acute illness; essential for guiding emergency respiratory/metabolic management.

sections.Killers First ‚ÄĒ What Will Kill Them In:
- **Bleeding/ pseudoaneurysm in anticoagulated patient** ‚Üí expanding haematoma ‚Üí neurovascular compromise. *Single finding:* loss of distal pulse or severe pain with pallor after puncture.
- **Systemic air embolism** (rare) ‚Üí sudden hypotension, altered consciousness, or cardiac arrest during/ immediately after puncture. *Single finding:* sudden neurological or cardiovascular collapse while the needle is in situ.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (prompting ABG):** acute dyspnoea, decreased SpO‚āā (<92%), altered mental state (confusion, drowsiness), shock, suspected acid‚Äďbase disturbance (Kussmaul breathing, tachypnoea), monitoring of therapy (ventilation, bicarbonate).
- **Key exam findings:** tachypnoea >20/min, use of accessory muscles, cyanosis, asterixis (CO‚āā retention), Kussmaul or Cheyne‚ÄĎStokes breathing pattern.
sections.Differential Diagnosis (Pattern Recognit:
- **Respiratory acidosis** (‚ÜĎpCO‚āā, ¬Ī‚ÜďHCO‚āÉ if chronic) ‚Üí hypoventilation: COPD, asthma, opioid overdose, chest wall disease.
- **Metabolic acidosis** (‚ÜďpH, ‚ÜďHCO‚āÉ) ‚Üí sepsis, DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, toxins.
- **Respiratory alkalosis** (‚ÜďpCO‚āā, ¬Ī‚ÜĎHCO‚āÉ if chronic) ‚Üí hyperventilation: anxiety, PE, hypoxia, salicylate overdose.
- **Metabolic alkalosis** (‚ÜĎpH, ‚ÜĎHCO‚āÉ) ‚Üí vomiting, diuretics, post‚ÄĎhypercapnia, hypokalaemia.
- **Mixed disorders** ‚Äď look at compensatory response; if compensation is ‚Äútoo little‚ÄĚ or ‚Äútoo much‚ÄĚ, suspect a second disorder.
- **Splitter:** pH + pCO‚āā + HCO‚āÉ. *Bedside test:* hold the values ‚Äď if pCO‚āā and HCO‚āÉ move in same direction ‚Üí likely mixed (e.g., high pCO‚āā + high HCO‚āÉ = respiratory acidosis + metabolic alkalosis). *Single clinical bifurcation:* check K‚Āļ ‚Äď low K‚Āļ suggests metabolic alkalosis or DKA; high K‚Āļ suggests renal failure or acidosis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Modified Allen‚Äôs test before radial puncture ‚Äď document result. (If abnormal, choose brachial/ femoral.)
  - Pre‚ÄĎprocedure check: full anticoagulation status (warfarin, DOAC, enoxaparin, unfractionated heparin).
  - Use sterile technique + 1% lidocaine local anaesthetic (0.1‚Äď0.3 mL subcutaneously [check local policy]).
  - Preferred site: radial artery (right or left). Use 23‚Äď25G needle, 30‚Äď45¬į angle, allow pulse to fill heparinised syringe.
  - Apply firm pressure 3‚Äď5 min (10 min if anticoagulated or after a failed attempt).
  - Analyse sample within 15 min (or place on ice ‚Äď stable 30‚Äď60 min).
- **Bloods:**
  - ABG panel: pH, pCO‚āā, pO‚āā, HCO‚āÉ, base excess, lactate, electrolytes (Na‚Āļ, K‚Āļ, Cl‚ĀĽ, Ca¬≤‚Āļ), glucose.
  - Simultaneous venous blood: full blood count, U&E, LFT, INR, blood cultures if sepsis.
- **Imaging:**
  - Not routinely required.
  - If suspect pseudoaneurysm or large haematoma ‚Üí urgent doppler ultrasound.
- **Special tests:**
  - Co‚ÄĎoximetry for carboxyhaemoglobin, methaemoglobin (if suspected inhalational injury, CO poisoning).
  - Arterial lactate ‚Äď key for sepsis, shock, ischaemia.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - Systematic approach: R.O.M.E. (Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal) or T.R.O.L.E. (To Remember, Opposite for respiratory, Equal for metabolic ‚Äď for pH and pCO‚āā).
  - Calculate anion gap: (Na‚Āļ ‚Äď [Cl‚ĀĽ + HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ]) ‚Üí normal 8‚Äď12. High gap = MUDPILES (Methanol, Uraemia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates).
  - Expected compensation formulas (e.g., Winter‚Äôs formula for metabolic acidosis: expected pCO‚āā = 1.5 √ó HCO‚āÉ + 8 ¬Ī 2).
- **Never‚ÄĎmiss test:** Simultaneous serum electrolytes (especially K‚Āļ) and lactate. *Missing hypokalaemia before correcting acidosis can cause fatal arrhythmia. Missing high lactate delays treating shock/ ischaemia.*
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Obtain verbal consent, perform Allen‚Äôs test.
  - Use local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% [check local policy]).
  - Technique: stabilise artery between two fingers; insert needle bevel up; flashback ‚Üí let pulse fill syringe slowly; no aspiration.
  - After removal: apply firm pressure for 3‚Äď5 minutes (10 min if anticoagulated), check distal pulses.
  - Send sample for analysis immediately.
  - Interpret results and act: e.g., severe acidosis (pH < 7.2) ‚Üí escalate; hypercapnic respiratory failure ‚Üí consider NIV; hypoxaemia ‚Üí increase FiO‚āā.
- **Ward (first 24‚Äď72 h):**
  - If frequent sampling needed (e.g., hourly blood gases in ventilated patient), consider insertion of arterial line (senior/skilled operator).
  - Document results in a flow chart (trending).
  - Adjust ventilator settings or oxygen therapy based on ABG.
- **Long‚ÄĎterm / outpatient:**
  - Not applicable for single procedure. For patients on long‚ÄĎterm oxygen, periodic ABG may be needed to assess type II respiratory failure.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM status not required for ABG unless part of a ventilated patient‚Äôs routine.
  - VTE prophylaxis: continue standard (no extra precautions for a single radial puncture).
  - Observation frequency: hourly distal circulation and puncture site check for 2‚Äď3 hours; then routine observations (NEWS2).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Lidocaine 1% (for local anaesthesia):** 0.1‚Äď0.3 mL subcutaneously (10‚Äď30 mg) [check local policy ‚Äď max dose 4.5 mg/kg without epinephrine].
- **Alternatives:** EMLA cream (30‚Äď60 min prior) for anxious patients; no local anaesthetic (if emergent ‚Äď but always offer).
- **Contraindications:** known allergy to amide local anaesthetics; infection at puncture site; poor collateral circulation (positive Allen‚Äôs test) ‚Äď use alternative site.
- **Renal/hepatic:** no dose adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Using too much lidocaine* ‚Üí toxicity (perioral numbness, seizures, arrhythmia) ‚Äď in practice <1 mL rarely causes this. *More common error:* not using enough pressure post‚ÄĎpuncture causing haematoma.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Haematoma (most common ‚Äď usually minor, resolves with 10‚ÄĎmin pressure).
  - Arterial spasm (self‚ÄĎlimiting; warm compress, stop attempts).
  - Pseudoaneurysm (if anticoagulated ‚Äď urgent ultrasound + senior).
  - Infection (rare ‚Äď use sterile technique).
  - Nerve injury (median/ radial nerve from stray needle ‚Äď paresthesia/ weakness; neurological review if persistent >24 h).
  - Air embolism (prevent by not drawing back on plunger, keeping system closed).
- **Chronic:**
  - Thrombosis of radial artery (usually asymptomatic due to collateral flow; document in notes to avoid future punctures).
  - Hand ischaemia (extremely rare if collateral flow normal ‚Äď escalate immediately).
sections.Prognosis: - Procedure‚ÄĎrelated morbidity <1%; most haematomas resolve without intervention. Temporary paraesthesia or vasovagal syncope occurs in <5%. Pseudoaneurysm may require surgical repair.
sections.Pearls (Positive Actions):
- **Always perform and document modified Allen‚Äôs test** ‚Äď if less than 5 seconds for colour return, use other side or brachial/ femoral artery.
- **Use a 25‚ÄĎgauge needle** ‚Äď reduces pain and vessel trauma; avoid larger needles (haemolysis, haematoma).
- **Warm the puncture site** if radial pulse is weak (hand in warm water 1‚Äď2 mins) ‚Äď improves success rate.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DO NOT use a plain (non‚ÄĎheparinised) syringe** ‚Äď sample will clot within seconds.
- **DO NOT send sample without eliminating air bubbles** ‚Äď air alters pO‚āā and pCO‚āā.
- **DO NOT delay analysis >15 min at room temperature** ‚Äď pCO‚āā and pH drift; use iced transport if unavoidable.
- **DO NOT ‚Äúdig‚ÄĚ for the artery after a failed attempt** ‚Äď withdraw completely, re‚ÄĎpalpate, and re‚ÄĎenter at a new site to avoid nerve/ tendon damage.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** oozing/ swelling at puncture site that doesn‚Äôt stop with 5‚ÄĎmin pressure; new severe pain, numbness or pallor of the hand; signs of infection (redness, pus, fever).
- **Follow‚ÄĎup:** none routinely required for a single uncomplicated ABG. If anticoagulated and large haematoma develops, arrange vascular outpatient follow‚ÄĎup within 1 week.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ ICU:** expanding haematoma causing neurovascular compromise (loss of radial pulse, severe pain, pallor, paraesthesia). Suspected pseudoaneurysm. Suspected air embolism (immediate senior + crash team).
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ‚Č• 5; lactate > 4 mmol/L; pH < 7.2 (or < 7.15 in ventilated patient); pCO‚āā > 10 kPa despite optimal medical therapy.
- **Complication:** any loss of distal pulse or motor function ‚Äď urgent vascular consult.

rawMarkdown:
## Arterial Blood Gas Sampling
**One-liner:** Bedside test for oxygenation, ventilation & acid-base status in acute illness; essential for guiding emergency respiratory/metabolic management.

### Killers First ‚ÄĒ What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Bleeding/ pseudoaneurysm in anticoagulated patient** ‚Üí expanding haematoma ‚Üí neurovascular compromise. *Single finding:* loss of distal pulse or severe pain with pallor after puncture.
- **Systemic air embolism** (rare) ‚Üí sudden hypotension, altered consciousness, or cardiac arrest during/ immediately after puncture. *Single finding:* sudden neurological or cardiovascular collapse while the needle is in situ.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (prompting ABG):** acute dyspnoea, decreased SpO‚āā (<92%), altered mental state (confusion, drowsiness), shock, suspected acid‚Äďbase disturbance (Kussmaul breathing, tachypnoea), monitoring of therapy (ventilation, bicarbonate).
- **Key exam findings:** tachypnoea >20/min, use of accessory muscles, cyanosis, asterixis (CO‚āā retention), Kussmaul or Cheyne‚ÄĎStokes breathing pattern.

### Differential Diagnosis (Pattern Recognition for Abnormal Results)
- **Respiratory acidosis** (‚ÜĎpCO‚āā, ¬Ī‚ÜďHCO‚āÉ if chronic) ‚Üí hypoventilation: COPD, asthma, opioid overdose, chest wall disease.
- **Metabolic acidosis** (‚ÜďpH, ‚ÜďHCO‚āÉ) ‚Üí sepsis, DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, toxins.
- **Respiratory alkalosis** (‚ÜďpCO‚āā, ¬Ī‚ÜĎHCO‚āÉ if chronic) ‚Üí hyperventilation: anxiety, PE, hypoxia, salicylate overdose.
- **Metabolic alkalosis** (‚ÜĎpH, ‚ÜĎHCO‚āÉ) ‚Üí vomiting, diuretics, post‚ÄĎhypercapnia, hypokalaemia.
- **Mixed disorders** ‚Äď look at compensatory response; if compensation is ‚Äútoo little‚ÄĚ or ‚Äútoo much‚ÄĚ, suspect a second disorder.
- **Splitter:** pH + pCO‚āā + HCO‚āÉ. *Bedside test:* hold the values ‚Äď if pCO‚āā and HCO‚āÉ move in same direction ‚Üí likely mixed (e.g., high pCO‚āā + high HCO‚āÉ = respiratory acidosis + metabolic alkalosis). *Single clinical bifurcation:* check K‚Āļ ‚Äď low K‚Āļ suggests metabolic alkalosis or DKA; high K‚Āļ suggests renal failure or acidosis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Modified Allen‚Äôs test before radial puncture ‚Äď document result. (If abnormal, choose brachial/ femoral.)
  - Pre‚ÄĎprocedure check: full anticoagulation status (warfarin, DOAC, enoxaparin, unfractionated heparin).
  - Use sterile technique + 1% lidocaine local anaesthetic (0.1‚Äď0.3 mL subcutaneously [check local policy]).
  - Preferred site: radial artery (right or left). Use 23‚Äď25G needle, 30‚Äď45¬į angle, allow pulse to fill heparinised syringe.
  - Apply firm pressure 3‚Äď5 min (10 min if anticoagulated or after a failed attempt).
  - Analyse sample within 15 min (or place on ice ‚Äď stable 30‚Äď60 min).
- **Bloods:**
  - ABG panel: pH, pCO‚āā, pO‚āā, HCO‚āÉ, base excess, lactate, electrolytes (Na‚Āļ, K‚Āļ, Cl‚ĀĽ, Ca¬≤‚Āļ), glucose.
  - Simultaneous venous blood: full blood count, U&E, LFT, INR, blood cultures if sepsis.
- **Imaging:**
  - Not routinely required.
  - If suspect pseudoaneurysm or large haematoma ‚Üí urgent doppler ultrasound.
- **Special tests:**
  - Co‚ÄĎoximetry for carboxyhaemoglobin, methaemoglobin (if suspected inhalational injury, CO poisoning).
  - Arterial lactate ‚Äď key for sepsis, shock, ischaemia.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - Systematic approach: R.O.M.E. (Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal) or T.R.O.L.E. (To Remember, Opposite for respiratory, Equal for metabolic ‚Äď for pH and pCO‚āā).
  - Calculate anion gap: (Na‚Āļ ‚Äď [Cl‚ĀĽ + HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ]) ‚Üí normal 8‚Äď12. High gap = MUDPILES (Methanol, Uraemia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates).
  - Expected compensation formulas (e.g., Winter‚Äôs formula for metabolic acidosis: expected pCO‚āā = 1.5 √ó HCO‚āÉ + 8 ¬Ī 2).
- **Never‚ÄĎmiss test:** Simultaneous serum electrolytes (especially K‚Āļ) and lactate. *Missing hypokalaemia before correcting acidosis can cause fatal arrhythmia. Missing high lactate delays treating shock/ ischaemia.*

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Obtain verbal consent, perform Allen‚Äôs test.
  - Use local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% [check local policy]).
  - Technique: stabilise artery between two fingers; insert needle bevel up; flashback ‚Üí let pulse fill syringe slowly; no aspiration.
  - After removal: apply firm pressure for 3‚Äď5 minutes (10 min if anticoagulated), check distal pulses.
  - Send sample for analysis immediately.
  - Interpret results and act: e.g., severe acidosis (pH < 7.2) ‚Üí escalate; hypercapnic respiratory failure ‚Üí consider NIV; hypoxaemia ‚Üí increase FiO‚āā.
- **Ward (first 24‚Äď72 h):**
  - If frequent sampling needed (e.g., hourly blood gases in ventilated patient), consider insertion of arterial line (senior/skilled operator).
  - Document results in a flow chart (trending).
  - Adjust ventilator settings or oxygen therapy based on ABG.
- **Long‚ÄĎterm / outpatient:**
  - Not applicable for single procedure. For patients on long‚ÄĎterm oxygen, periodic ABG may be needed to assess type II respiratory failure.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM status not required for ABG unless part of a ventilated patient‚Äôs routine.
  - VTE prophylaxis: continue standard (no extra precautions for a single radial puncture).
  - Observation frequency: hourly distal circulation and puncture site check for 2‚Äď3 hours; then routine observations (NEWS2).

### Drugs & Doses
- **Lidocaine 1% (for local anaesthesia):** 0.1‚Äď0.3 mL subcutaneously (10‚Äď30 mg) [check local policy ‚Äď max dose 4.5 mg/kg without epinephrine].
- **Alternatives:** EMLA cream (30‚Äď60 min prior) for anxious patients; no local anaesthetic (if emergent ‚Äď but always offer).
- **Contraindications:** known allergy to amide local anaesthetics; infection at puncture site; poor collateral circulation (positive Allen‚Äôs test) ‚Äď use alternative site.
- **Renal/hepatic:** no dose adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Using too much lidocaine* ‚Üí toxicity (perioral numbness, seizures, arrhythmia) ‚Äď in practice <1 mL rarely causes this. *More common error:* not using enough pressure post‚ÄĎpuncture causing haematoma.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Haematoma (most common ‚Äď usually minor, resolves with 10‚ÄĎmin pressure).
  - Arterial spasm (self‚ÄĎlimiting; warm compress, stop attempts).
  - Pseudoaneurysm (if anticoagulated ‚Äď urgent ultrasound + senior).
  - Infection (rare ‚Äď use sterile technique).
  - Nerve injury (median/ radial nerve from stray needle ‚Äď paresthesia/ weakness; neurological review if persistent >24 h).
  - Air embolism (prevent by not drawing back on plunger, keeping system closed).
- **Chronic:**
  - Thrombosis of radial artery (usually asymptomatic due to collateral flow; document in notes to avoid future punctures).
  - Hand ischaemia (extremely rare if collateral flow normal ‚Äď escalate immediately).

### Prognosis
- Procedure‚ÄĎrelated morbidity <1%; most haematomas resolve without intervention. Temporary paraesthesia or vasovagal syncope occurs in <5%. Pseudoaneurysm may require surgical repair.

### Pearls (Positive Actions)
- **Always perform and document modified Allen‚Äôs test** ‚Äď if less than 5 seconds for colour return, use other side or brachial/ femoral artery.
- **Use a 25‚ÄĎgauge needle** ‚Äď reduces pain and vessel trauma; avoid larger needles (haemolysis, haematoma).
- **Warm the puncture site** if radial pulse is weak (hand in warm water 1‚Äď2 mins) ‚Äď improves success rate.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **DO NOT use a plain (non‚ÄĎheparinised) syringe** ‚Äď sample will clot within seconds.
- **DO NOT send sample without eliminating air bubbles** ‚Äď air alters pO‚āā and pCO‚āā.
- **DO NOT delay analysis >15 min at room temperature** ‚Äď pCO‚āā and pH drift; use iced transport if unavoidable.
- **DO NOT ‚Äúdig‚ÄĚ for the artery after a failed attempt** ‚Äď withdraw completely, re‚ÄĎpalpate, and re‚ÄĎenter at a new site to avoid nerve/ tendon damage.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** oozing/ swelling at puncture site that doesn‚Äôt stop with 5‚ÄĎmin pressure; new severe pain, numbness or pallor of the hand; signs of infection (redness, pus, fever).
- **Follow‚ÄĎup:** none routinely required for a single uncomplicated ABG. If anticoagulated and large haematoma develops, arrange vascular outpatient follow‚ÄĎup within 1 week.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ ICU:** expanding haematoma causing neurovascular compromise (loss of radial pulse, severe pain, pallor, paraesthesia). Suspected pseudoaneurysm. Suspected air embolism (immediate senior + crash team).
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ‚Č• 5; lactate > 4 mmol/L; pH < 7.2 (or < 7.15 in ventilated patient); pCO‚āā > 10 kPa despite optimal medical therapy.
- **Complication:** any loss of distal pulse or motor function ‚Äď urgent vascular consult.
